paragraphs.body: A Cross-Cultural View on the “Lost in Translation” of Chinese Poetry  The “lost in translation” Phenomenon of Chinese Poetry Chinese poetry has a deep cultural heritage and unique artistic style. It is an important part of Chinese literature and holds a special position in the international literary exchange perspective. When Chinese poems are translated into English, the translated works are somewhat “lost in translation”, which often leads to the loss of the original poetry's linguistic charm, cultural connotation, and philosophical depth. These losses may not serve as keys in other language versions, however, they do have a huge effect on the cross-cultural communication of poetry and its global influence (Pozzana, 2007; Zhu, 2022).

paragraphs.body: The missing elements are mainly reflected in three aspects. One is subtle differences in language, the second is obstacles to the transformation of cultural symbols, and the last is the weakening of philosophical implications (Edmond, 2023). Since scholars continually delve into the subject of Chinese poetry translation, this paper will induct the missing elements by exploring their works and the poetry of phenomenal poets such as Bei Dao.

Language as Words Using in Translation Language is the primary obstacle in poetry translation, especially the unique voice rhythm, polysemy, and grammatical structure in contemporary Chinese poetry, which makes the English translation process face huge challenges. In Bei Dao’s poem “Cold Hope”, the line “the earth is flying away/the drawn-back horizon” reveals a dialectical reflection on truth and nothingness with abstract language and open imagery (Pozzana, 2007). In translated texts, the compactness of this language is often weakened by the lack of corresponding structure in English.

annotations.comment: For direct quotes, please indicate the page number in the citation.

paragraphs.body: In addition, “the sounds of bells announcing the time” in Bei Dao’s poem not only conveys a sense of the passage of time but also metaphorically represents philosophical reflections on history and existence (Zhu, 2022). However, in English translation, this multiple semantic transmission is often limited by the expression form of the target language, which is the limitation of the English text context. The intonation and rhythm in Chinese poetry are easily lost in translation. For example, the tension created by punctuation and blank space in Bei Dao’s poems is often simplified into ordinary narrative language in the English translation (Edmond, 2023). This language loss directly affects the poem's ideological depth and artistic appeal.

The Transformation of Cultural Symbols Cultural symbols are the second biggest challenge in poetry translation. Many images in Chinese poetry are deeply rooted in specific cultural traditions, and transmitting their meaning requires a full understanding of the cultural background. The frequent appearance of "metal" and "rose" in Bei Dao's late poems not only symbolizes his loneliness and struggle in life as an exiled poet, but also implies a deep reflection on identity (Zhu, 2022). However, these images may be simplified into a single material symbol in English translation, making it difficult to convey their complex cultural connotations (Ming, 2023).

When Chinese classical poetry enters the Western context, it is often rewritten due to the translator's subjective aesthetic preferences. For example, although Ezra Pound's "Cathay" (Huaxia Collection) promoted the popularity of Tang poetry in the West, it also changed the cultural context of the original poem (Ming, 2023). Similar problems still exist in the translation of contemporary poetry: "The metal rose never dies" in Bei Dao's poem may be interpreted as a symbol of tenacity in translation, but the cultural struggle and philosophical speculation behind it are often overlooked.

Knowing the value of Philosophical Implications The philosophical implications of Chinese poetry are an important part of its artistic value, but in translation, these profound philosophical reflections are often difficult to convey due to cultural and linguistic barriers. The "sense of distance" that runs through Bei Dao's poetry is one of his core philosophical concepts, which is manifested in the poet's reflection on reality and existence through abstract images and profound language (Pozzana, 2007). For example, in "Cold Hope", the combination of "cold" and "hope" reveals the philosophical meaning of binary opposition through irony. However, this philosophical speculation loses its subtlety in English translation due to the tendency of literal translation (Qin et al., 2023).

The holistic view and harmonious aesthetics in Chinese philosophy are often simplified into linear logic in the Western context during English translation (Qin et al., 2023). Bei Dao's description of "time" in his poems is deeply connected to the time view of Chinese Taoist philosophy, but translation often fails to convey this philosophical connection to readers due to the lack of annotations or cultural background supplements.

Perspectives and Solutions for reducing the loss in translation Although there are losses in the process of translating modern Chinese poetry in the West, some scholars believe that creative adaptations in translation have helped promote the international dissemination of Chinese poetry. For example, Ezra Pound's "Cathay" based on classical Chinese Tang poetry not only made Tang poetry appear in Western culture but also influenced the creation of Western modernist poetry (Ming, 2023). Similarly, some translators make Chinese poetry more acceptable to Western readers through imagery enhancement. However such adaptations often lead to the loss of the original cultural depth. For example, Pound translated the Confucian philosophy of "日日新" into "make it new", which strengthened its connection with modernity but ignored its local philosophical connotation (Ming, 2023). Translation should seek a balance between fidelity and innovation. The adaptation that is too biased towards the target language may weaken the artistry and cultural uniqueness of the original work, which in turn harms the international dissemination of Chinese poetry. (Klein, L. 2024)

To reduce losses in translation, the "three beauties" principle of ecological translation studies can be adopted, namely the unity of formal beauty, rhyme beauty, and artistic conception beauty (Qin et al., 2023). For example, adding cultural background and philosophical connotations through annotated translation can help improve the accuracy and readability of the translation. In addition, collaborative translation between translators and poets can better achieve a complete presentation of the complexity of poetry (Ming, 2023). Practice has proved that this strategy has achieved good results in some translations. For example, the English translation of Bei Dao's poetry collection "Notes from the City of the Sun" helps readers understand the cultural and philosophical implications of his poems more deeply through detailed cultural annotations. References Edmond, J. (2023). Literature as translation: Bei Dao beyond world poetry. PRISM: Theory and Modern Chinese Literature, 20(1), 139-151. Klein, L. (2024). The translational turn and the dual pressures on Chinese literary studies. The Cambridge Journal of Postcolonial Literary Inquiry, 1-20. https://doi.org/10.1017/pli.2024.8 Ming, D. (2023, August 31). 影响与焦虑：中国当代诗在美国的译介状况 [Influence and anxiety: The status of contemporary Chinese poetry translation in America]. Southern Poetry. Pozzana, C. (2007). Distances of poetry: An introduction to Bei Dao. Positions: East Asia Cultures Critique, 15(1), 91-111. Qin, Z., Cui, X., & Gao, X. (2023). The characteristics of contemporary Chinese translation theory development: A systematic review of studies in core Chinese journals (2012-2022). Humanities and Social Sciences Communications, 10(596), 1-10. https://doi.org/10.1057/s41599-023-01955-w Zhu, Y. (2022). Foreignness in the imagery of Bei Dao's later poetry. SHS Web of Conferences, 148, Article 01016. https://doi.org/10.1051/shsconf/202214801016
